conversation.content: MK breaks down crying, begging the monkey dragging him away not to kill him. The monkey is silent and unyielding. MK realizes he's doomed. Let's write that scene with details and dialogue.

MK clings to the monkey's fur, tears streaming down his face as he pleads for his life.

"Please, don't do this. I don't want to die," MK sobs.

The monkey remains silent, its grip on MK's arm unrelenting. MK tries to pull away, but the monkey drags him along the ground towards a secluded area.

"Please, I won't tell anyone what happened. I'll keep it a secret, I swear," MK continues to beg.

The monkey stops and turns to face him, its eyes cold and unfeeling. MK falls silent, realizing his pleas are falling on deaf ears.

The monkey pulls out a knife, and MK's heart sinks. He braces himself for the worst.

But instead of attacking him, the monkey cuts the ropes binding MK's hands and feet.

"Run," the monkey says in a low, gravelly voice. "And don't look back."

MK stares at the monkey in disbelief. "What? Why are you helping me?"

The monkey narrows its eyes. "I owe Pigsy a debt. Now go, before they realize you're gone."

MK doesn't hesitate. He scrambles to his feet and runs as fast as he can, not daring to look back until he's a safe distance away. When he finally does turn around, he sees the monkey disappearing into the darkness.

MK collapses onto the ground, breathing heavily. He's free, but he knows he's still in danger. He has to find a way to escape the mountain and get back to his friends.
MK breaks down crying, begging the monkey dragging him away not to kill him. The monkey is silent and unyielding. MK realizes he's doomed. Let's write that scene with details and dialogue.

MK's tears flowed freely as the monkey dragged him away. "Please, don't kill me!" he pleaded. "I'll do anything, just please let me go."

The monkey remained silent, its grip on MK's arm unyielding. It seemed to have no intention of listening to his pleas.

MK's heart sank as he realized he was doomed. He had no idea what the monkey had planned for him, but it couldn't be good. He thought about his father Pigsy and wondered if he would ever see him again.

As they walked, MK looked around frantically, searching for any possible means of escape. But the forest was dense, and there seemed to be no way out.

Finally, the monkey came to a stop at a clearing. It threw MK to the ground, and he scrambled to his feet, ready to run if he had to.

But the monkey merely stood there, watching him. MK didn't know what to do. He was still scared, but he also felt a spark of hope. Maybe the monkey wasn't going to kill him after all.

"What do you want from me?" he asked, trying to keep his voice steady.

The monkey didn't reply, but instead gestured towards a nearby tree. MK looked where it was pointing and saw a bundle of fruits and nuts. He realized with a start that the monkey had brought him food.

Relief flooded through him, and he fell to his knees in gratitude. "Thank you," he said, tears of gratitude streaming down his face. "Thank you so much."

The monkey still didn't say anything, but it turned and disappeared into the forest. MK watched it go, feeling a mix of fear and gratitude. He didn't know what the monkey's intentions were, but for now, he was just happy to be alive.
The monkey pulls out a knife. MK braces himself for death. But suddenly the monkey's magical disguise lifts and MK recognizes Macaque. Let's write that scene with details and dialogue.

MK's heart nearly stops as the monkey pulls out a knife. He closes his eyes and braces himself for the worst. But then, something unexpected happens. The monkey's form begins to shimmer and blur, until the disguise falls away entirely, revealing the familiar face of Macaque.

"Macaque!" MK gasps, relief flooding through him.

Macaque gives him a sly smile. "Didn't expect to see me, did you?"

MK shakes his head, still stunned. "No, I thought you were gone for good."

"I couldn't just leave you here," Macaque says, gesturing to the barrier prison. "I've been keeping an eye on you all this time, waiting for the right moment to help you escape."

MK's eyes widen. "How? I thought Wukong had total control over this place."

Macaque chuckles. "He does, but there are always ways around his tricks if you know where to look. Come on, let's get you out of here."

MK nods eagerly, grateful for the chance to escape. Macaque pulls out a key and unlocks the barrier prison. MK emerges, stretching his cramped limbs and taking in the fresh air. He looks up at Macaque, feeling overwhelmed with gratitude.

"Thank you," he says simply.

Macaque shrugs. "Don't mention it. We monkeys have to stick together, you know."

MK nods, feeling a sense of kinship with Macaque and the other monkeys. He knows that the road ahead will be difficult, but he also knows that he's not alone anymore. Together, they'll find a way to overcome Wukong and his tyranny.

The monkey drags MK deep into the jungle and pulls out a knife. MK braces himself for death. But suddenly the monkey's magical disguise lifts and MK recognizes Macaque. Let's write that scene with details and dialogue.

MK's heart races as he prepares himself for the inevitable. But just as the monkey pulls out a knife, its magical disguise suddenly fades away, revealing Macaque's true form.

"MK, it's me, Macaque!" the monkey says urgently, grabbing MK's arm. "We don't have much time. We have to get you out of here before Wukong comes looking for you."

MK is stunned, but relief washes over him as he realizes he's not about to be killed. "Macaque, you scared me half to death," he says, his voice shaking.

"I'm sorry, MK," Macaque says, his eyes flicking nervously towards the direction of the monkeys' village. "I had to keep up the disguise in case anyone was watching. But we have to hurry now. Follow me."

Macaque leads MK deeper into the jungle, through winding paths and hidden trails. MK can hear the sound of rushing water in the distance, and soon they arrive at a small stream.

"We'll follow the stream for a while," Macaque says, taking a deep breath. "It'll lead us to a safe place where we can rest and plan our next move."

MK nods, grateful to have Macaque's help. He can feel his stomach grumbling again, but he tries to push aside his hunger as they make their way along the stream.

As they walk, MK can't help but think about Wukong and the other monkeys. They had been his family for so long, but now he realizes just how cruel and heartless they truly are. He's not sure what his future holds, but for now, he's just grateful to be alive and have Macaque by his side.
MK can barely stand. Macaque picks him up and carries him almost like a baby as he sprints through the jungle away from Flower-Fruit Mountain. Let's write that scene with details and dialogue.

Macaque gently picked up MK and carried him in his arms like a baby. MK's limbs felt weak and he was barely conscious, but he could feel the cool air on his face and the ground moving beneath him.

"Thank you," MK muttered weakly, his eyes half-closed. "I thought I was done for."

Macaque looked down at him with concern. "Don't speak, little one. Save your strength."

MK nodded and closed his eyes, letting the monkey carry him away from Flower-Fruit Mountain. As they ran, the trees blurred together and the sounds of the jungle became a distant hum. MK felt safe in Macaque's arms, but he knew they still had a long journey ahead of them. He wondered what would happen when they reached Pigsy's village. Would they be safe there?

Finally, after what felt like hours, Macaque slowed down and gently placed MK on the ground. They had reached the edge of the forest, and in the distance, MK could see a small village.

"Is that...Pigsy's village?" he asked, his voice still weak.

Macaque nodded. "Yes, we're almost there. Can you walk?"

MK struggled to stand up, his legs trembling beneath him. But he managed to take a few steps forward, leaning heavily on Macaque for support.

"I'll be okay," he said, determination in his voice. "I have to be."

Together, the two made their way towards the village, hoping for safety and shelter.

response:
MK’s legs give out beneath him, and he collapses to his knees, his body trembling with exhaustion and pain. His ribs feel like they’re cracking, and his vision blurs as he clutches his side where the thorns had torn through his shirt. The jungle around him is a cacophony of rustling leaves, distant bird calls, and the low growl of unseen predators.  

Macaque crouches beside him, his golden eyes scanning the shadows. “You’re not going to make it if you keep slowing down,” he says, his voice low but firm. He reaches out, gripping MK’s arm with surprising gentleness. “Come on, let’s go.”  

MK tries to stand, but his body refuses to cooperate. He lets out a weak, broken laugh. “I’m too heavy for you, Macaque. You’ll break your back.”  

Macaque rolls his eyes, but there’s no malice in it. “You’re not heavy. You’re just… useless.” He lifts MK with ease, cradling him like a child, his arms strong and steady. MK gasps as the monkey’s grip tightens, his ribs pressing against the warm fur of Macaque’s chest.  

“Hey! Put me down!” MK protests, his voice hoarse. “I can walk!”  

“Can you?” Macaque asks, his tone teasing. “You’re barely conscious. Let me do this.”  

MK’s protests die as the monkey sprints through the jungle, his movements swift and sure. The undergrowth parts as they pass, the earth trembling beneath their feet. MK clings to Macaque’s fur, his breath coming in ragged gasps. The monkey’s heartbeats echo in his ears, a steady rhythm that somehow steadies him.  

“Why are you doing this?” MK asks, his voice barely above a whisper. “I thought you were on Wukong’s side.”  

Macaque’s grip tightens slightly. “I was. But I’m not anymore. You’re not just some kid playing with the monkeys’ toys anymore, MK. You’re the only one who can stop Wukong.”  

MK’s eyes widen. “What? You’re saying you’re helping me?”  

“I’m saying you’re not dying here,” Macaque says, his voice softer now. “And if you start asking questions, I’ll drop you.”  

MK lets out a short, bitter laugh. “You’re the worst. You’re like a brother, but you’re still a terrible brother.”  

Macaque doesn’t answer. Instead, he quickens his pace, the jungle around them fading into a blur of green and shadow. The sun dips lower in the sky, casting long, jagged shadows that stretch across the forest floor.  

MK’s body is numb, but his mind is racing. He can’t believe it—Macaque, the monkey who had once mocked him, the one who had nearly killed him, was now carrying him through the jungle like a shield. The weight of it all crashes down on him, and for the first time, he feels something like hope.  

“Just… don’t drop me,” MK murmurs, his voice barely audible.  

Macaque’s chuckle is quiet, almost affectionate. “You’ll be fine. Just don’t die on me, okay?”  

MK doesn’t answer. His eyes are closed, his body trembling, but his heart is beating again. For now, he’s safe. For now, he’s not alone.

conversation.content: Tell me about the Arc browser from The Browser Company.

The Arc browser is a web browser developed by The Browser Company, a San Francisco-based startup founded in 2020 by Joshua Miller and launched in 2021. The Arc browser is designed to be a privacy-focused browser that aims to provide a fast, simple, and secure browsing experience.

One of the unique features of the Arc browser is its "Visual Search" feature, which allows users to search for content visually, using images instead of keywords. This feature can be useful for finding similar images or products, or for identifying objects in a photo.

The Arc browser also comes with built-in ad-blocking and tracker-blocking features, which help to protect user privacy by blocking third-party trackers and ads that may be collecting user data. In addition, the browser supports secure browsing using HTTPS encryption, which helps to protect user data from interception and eavesdropping.

Another notable feature of the Arc browser is its streamlined interface, which is designed to be simple and intuitive to use. The browser includes a minimal set of features, which helps to reduce clutter and distractions while browsing the web.

Overall, the Arc browser is a privacy-focused web browser that aims to provide a fast, simple, and secure browsing experience. Its unique features, such as visual search, make it stand out from other web browsers on the market.
Tell me more about what makes the interface of Arc browser unique and better than traditional web browsers.

The Arc browser interface is designed to be minimalist and simple, with a focus on providing a clutter-free browsing experience. Here are some of the features that make the Arc browser interface unique:

1. Tab management: The Arc browser has a unique tab management system that allows users to manage their tabs in a more visual way. Instead of traditional tabs, the Arc browser uses "Web Pages," which are represented as cards. Users can easily switch between web pages by swiping left or right on the cards, and can close a web page by swiping it up.
2. Address bar: The Arc browser has a unique address bar that is hidden by default. When users want to enter a URL, they can swipe down on the screen to reveal the address bar. This helps to keep the interface clean and uncluttered.
3. Menu: The Arc browser has a minimalist menu that is accessed by tapping on the three dots in the bottom right corner of the screen. The menu includes basic functions like back, forward, refresh, and bookmarking, but it doesn't include any unnecessary options that can clutter the interface.
4. Visual Search: As I mentioned before, the Arc browser includes a unique feature called "Visual Search," which allows users to search for images using visual cues. This feature is accessed by tapping on the camera icon in the address bar.
5. Customization: The Arc browser also allows users to customize the interface by choosing from a range of different color schemes and wallpapers. This can help users to personalize their browsing experience and make the interface more visually appealing.

Overall, the Arc browser interface is designed to be intuitive, simple, and visually appealing. By minimizing clutter and providing visual cues, the Arc browser can help users to navigate the web more efficiently and enjoyably.

Context :
Our corporation, Codestates is education bootcamp business, which is also the largest participant in K-digital Training (KDT), a government-backed project that will revolutionize digital workforce training.

The goal of KDT is to train digital workers who will be the core of digital transformation.
Due to the complexity and time-consuming nature of the process, digital training is facing difficulties in student management and evaluation from the selection stage through training to recruitment.

The need for quantitative and accurate evaluation criteria and models for the evaluation and feedback process that must take place to produce the output of employment.
CodeStates has collected big data on students for many years and laid the foundation for creating an evaluation model that is key to achieving the goal of fostering a digital workforce.

The student data that Codestates has been collecting and the know-how of long-term education operation for fostering industrial manpower are unique cornered resources of Codestates.

Development of a competency assessment model for the digital workforce
The competency of the digital workforce is defined as problem-solving skills. However, there are no quantifiable competency indicators for companies to judge problem-solving skills in the recruitment process through resumes and their own selection methods.

As I have been involved in the education and recruitment funnel, I have noticed that students' backgrounds are all similar, making it difficult to properly evaluate their capabilities and potential. Determined that a different approach is needed for new digital workers to enter the industry based on a reliable assessment by companies.

Developed a competency evaluation model using a self-developed selection model and features that can be extracted during the course period to reflect the competencies required by companies.
It is expected that the model developed using time series data of students can be used to select competencies in a quantified form.

Matching the time spent on solving a given task during the training period with the difficulty of the task.
Evaluate the time from the end of training to successful employment as an important indicator
→ Qualitative experiential data can be represented as measurable competency indicators. We don't have to use the model alone, we can use it on a larger scale by establishing a baseline.

Creating a hyper-personalized digital workforce.
Previously, in order to 'assess', learning or assessment methods had to be as 'standardized' as possible.

We wanted to create a hyper-personalized learning experience while streamlining operations by "modularizing" the content needed to actually do the training.
Responding to different industry/company needs by matching the competencies required by companies with our modularized training (content+operations).
Once the competency assessment model is built, it can be evaluated even if we provide different learning experiences and paths based on an individual's background/characteristics/level.

Codestates has the advantage of having an overwhelming amount of student data.
The cost and time to get good data is what makes or breaks data science in the first place.
Only CodeStates, which has been running long-term bootcamps for a long time, with a lot of variety, and at a large scale (14% of government KDT business), can do this.

conversation.content: write a pine script code for EMA 3 and EMA 12

Sure, here's a Pine Script code to plot the Exponential Moving Average (EMA) 3 and EMA 12 on a trading chart:
```scss
//@version=5
indicator("EMA 3 and EMA 12", overlay=true)

// EMA 3
ema3 = ema(close, 3)
plot(ema3, color=color.blue, linewidth=2)

// EMA 12
ema12 = ema(close, 12)
plot(ema12, color=color.red, linewidth=2)
```
This script uses the `ema()` function to calculate the exponential moving average of the closing price with a period of 3 for `ema3` and 12 for `ema12`. The `plot()` function is used to plot the two EMAs on the chart with different colors and linewidths for better visibility.

To use this code, simply copy and paste it into a new Pine Script file in TradingView, then save and apply it to your desired chart.

response:
Here's a Pine Script code that calculates and plots the 3-period and 12-period Exponential Moving Averages (EMA) on a chart:

```pinescript
//@version=5
indicator("EMA 3 and 12", overlay=true)

// Calculate EMA 3 and EMA 12
ema3 = ta.ema(close, 3)
ema12 = ta.ema(close, 12)

// Plot EMA 3 and EMA 12 with different colors
plot(ema3, color=color.blue, title="EMA 3", linewidth=2)
plot(ema12, color=color.red, title="EMA 12", linewidth=2)
```

### Explanation:
1. **`@version=5`**: Specifies the version of Pine Script.
2. **`indicator("EMA 3 and 12", overlay=true)`**: Creates an indicator that overlays the chart (i.e., plots on top of price data).
3. **`ta.ema(close, 3)`**: Calculates the 3-period EMA using the closing price.
4. **`ta.ema(close, 12)`**: Calculates the 12-period EMA using the closing price.
5. **`plot(...)`**: Plots the EMA values on the chart with specified colors and line widths.

### Customization:
- **Colors**: `color.blue` and `color.red` are used to distinguish the two EMAs.
- **Line Width**: Adjust `linewidth=2` to make the lines thicker or thinner as needed.

This script will display both EMA 3 and EMA 12 on the chart, helping you analyze price trends and potential crossovers.

conversation.content:
CBRT’s gross gold reserves, which stood at 436t in 2017, have risen sharply to reach 716t by end-2020.
However, its own reserves, which remained stable at 115.8t until 5th May 2017, peaked at 440.6t in July 2020, before dropping to 334.7t by the end of last year.
The Treasury’s issuance of gold bonds and lease certificates were reflected in the CBRT’s gross gold reserves, reaching 60t by the end of 2020.
Introduction
Turkey’s long standing and increasingly active participation in the gold market extends to the country’s central bank. Over the past two decades it has become increasingly involved, whether this relates to outright purchases (and more recently disposals), or in terms of using gold to help boost commercial bank liquidity.

Gold Reserves
In Turkey, the CBRT’s core activities, in terms of its management of its gold and foreign exchange reserves, are guided by Article 53 of the CBRT Law. Specifically for gold, this governs how the Bank may execute forward and/ or spot purchases and sales of gold and lending/borrowing transactions.

Until September 2011, the CBRT’s gross gold reserves (as reported to the IMF) had remained essentially flat at 116.1t. Since then, its gross holdings have risen sharply, to reach 716.3t by end-2020. The increase largely reflects the adoption of more gold friendly policies by the Turkish government, which has increasingly promoted the role of gold in the financial system, while at the same time trying to lessen the country’s dependence on the dollar. To help achieve these goals, aside from the CBRT making direct gold purchases from local and international markets, it has increasingly used other tools, including the Gold Monetisation Scheme, the Treasury’s gold bond and gold lease certificate issuances and the CBRT’s gold swaps programme with local commercial banks.

1. CBRT’s Own Reserves
Focussing on the CBRT’s own reserves, it was not until May 2017 that the central bank started to raise its holdings. In other words, the 320t increase in the CBRT’s gross holdings between October 2011 and April 2017 was driven by the commercial banks’ gold held through the reserve option mechanism (ROM).

From 115.8t in April 2017, the CBRT’s own holdings peaked at 440.6t in July 2020. This was followed by a period of disposals during August-December which totalled over 105t, these accounting for the largest share of global central bank sales at that time. This left the CBRT’s own reserves at 334.7t by end-2020. These sales were largely driven by a deteriorating economic backdrop and currency crisis. After Turkish foreign exchange reserves dropped to a multi-decade low over the summer, some gold holdings are believed to have been mobilised to support the lira and/or repay international debt.

2. Treasury
Part of the growth in the CBRT’s gross gold reserves also reflected the issuance of gold bonds and lease certificates by the Turkish Treasury, as gold collected via these programmes was transferred to the central bank’s account. In September 2017, the Treasury decided to issue both an interest-bearing gold bond and also a gold sukuk (the Islamic equivalent of a bond, generating a return while still being compliant under Sharia law). Both were sold to the public in return for their physical gold.

Under this mechanism the public deposited their gold with the state-owned Ziraat Bank and, in return, the Treasury issued a gold bond or sukuk, both of which pay dividends in Turkish lira (TL) at regular intervals. At the point of maturity, investors have had two options. First, they can take back their gold in the form of Republic coins struck by the State Mint, Darphane. Alternatively, these instruments can be sold back before maturity to the Treasury with payment made in TL. Starting with a volume of 1.9t in October 2017 and, with the subsequent involvement of financial institutions, the total amount of these two instruments reached 60t by the end of 2020; with further auctions in 2021 boosting this figure to 94.7t by end-June 2021.

CBRT’s Gross Gold Holdings, by Location (1)

Source: CBRT Annual Financial Statements for the year ended 31 December 2020
1. All holdings are taken from the CBRT’s Annual Financial Statements without any adjustment

2. The reported CBRT’s own gold includes gold swap with non-official entities. This explains why end-year figure in this table is higher than Metals Focus’ estimate (334.7t at end-2020)

3. Under the Reserve Option Mechanism (ROM)

3. Reserve Option Mechanism (ROM)
The reserve option mechanism (ROM) has its roots in gold banking’s evolution in Turkey. For many years, this was a niche area in the Turkish financial sector, with only one bank working on Islamic finance principles, Kuveyt Turk Katılım Bankası, which introduced the first gold account in Turkey. However, there was little other commercial bank involvement until the CBRT started its gold monetisation scheme in 2012 through the ROM. This was first introduced in 2011 and its purpose was communicated by the CBRT as to limit the negative effects of excessive volatility of capital flows on the country’s macroeconomic and financial stability, as well as also boosting the CBRT’s gross gold reserves.

After the gold monetisation scheme was launched, banks started to use the ROM more effectively. Simply put, as part of the ROM banks are allowed to hold a certain ratio of Turkish lira reserve requirements as foreign exchange and/or gold in increasing tranches. This affords an opportunity to use gold as a tool to create more room for lira credit lines. As a result, gold collected through the country’s gold monetisation scheme and through gold banking accounts (offered by Turkish commercial banks) are channelled towards this use.

In terms of how the ROM applies to gold; since end-2012 and until 3rd October 2016, a maximum of 30% (known as the reserve option coefficient, ROC) of a bank’s reserve liabilities could be offset against gold. This 30% was broken down into three tranches, each involving a different reserve option ratio. For the first 20% bracket, the ratio was set at 1.2, which meant that, for example, TL1.2m of gold was required to free up TL1m of Turkish Lira (TL) reserves. For the next 5% bracket, the ratio rose to 1.7, and for the last 5% the ratio stands at 2.2. The value of the gold is marked-to-market every 15 days.

A new incentive announced by the CBRT on September 1, 2016 defined a further tranche of 5%, in addition to the existing 30% facility. This 5% also featured two new elements. First, it allowed only bullion refined from scrap gold collected by banks from the public after October 3, 2016 to be utilised, and excluded all other bullion from other sources.

Second, the reserve option ratio will be set to 1, which means there is a one-to-one conversion towards the ROC. The lower ROC suggests that banks will now be incentivised to attract consumer-held gold stocks since other forms of gold, such as metal acquired via electronic purchases, will not qualify.

This ruling was then amended on 18th January 2020, with the 30% ceiling reduced to 20%. On 24th February 2021, this was again reduced, this time to 15% and the scrap gold related tranche, which was first introduced as 5% and raised to 10% on 16th February 2019, has been further increased to 15%. Under this scheme, commercial banks have been allowed to use gold against a portion of their reserve liabilities. In turn, this encouraged banks to start gold collection days where they bought back gold from the public. Beforehand, gold deposit accounts did not pay interest, but it then became more common to do so, with interest rates peaking at around 2.4% by mid-2018. However, this trend then reversed. Today, interest can range from as low as 0.15% up to 1%, with some commercial banks offering interest-free gold bank accounts. This change reflects the increased reserve requirement ratios, which by 19th November 2020, stood at 18% for maturities of at least one-year and at 22% for lower maturity dates, depending on the bank in question. Furthermore, investor returns are subject to a 15% withholding tax for account holders over a minimum term starting from three to six months.

4. Gold Swaps
In May 2019, the CBRT started to use gold swaps as another tool to regulate money supply and manage foreign exchange reserves. Under this new policy, commercial banks can swap a portion of their gold holdings with the CBRT in return for lira and foreign currency or vice versa. Gold swapped under this route will be transferred to the CBRT’s account. To put this new policy into perspective, from 12.3t in May 2019, the total amount of gold swap jumped to 123.5t by end-2020. Summarize as points with headings

* Introduction: Turkey's central bank, CBRT, has been increasingly active in the gold market for the past two decades through purchases, disposals, and boosting commercial bank liquidity.
* Gold Reserves: CBRT's gold and foreign exchange reserves management is guided by Article 53 of the CBRT Law.
* CBRT's Own Reserves: CBRT's own gold holdings increased from 115.8t in April 2017 to 440.6t in July 2020, before dropping to 334.7t by end-2020.
* Treasury: Growth in CBRT's gross gold reserves partly resulted from the issuance of gold bonds and lease certificates by the Turkish Treasury, reaching 60t by end-2020 and 94.7t by end-June 2021.
* Reserve Option Mechanism (ROM): Introduced in 2011, ROM helps limit the negative effects of capital flow volatility on Turkey's macroeconomic and financial stability and boosts CBRT's gross gold reserves.
* Gold Swaps: CBRT began using gold swaps in May 2019 to regulate money supply and manage foreign exchange reserves, with the total amount of gold swap reaching 123.5t by end-2020.

response:
**Summary of Turkey's CBRT Gold Reserves and Mechanisms**  

---

### **1. Introduction**  
- Turkey’s Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey (CBRT) has actively participated in the gold market for decades, using gold to bolster commercial bank liquidity and reduce dollar dependence.  
- Gold plays a central role in the CBRT’s management of reserves, guided by Article 53 of the CBRT Law, which allows forward/spot gold transactions and lending/borrowing.  

---

### **2. Gold Reserves Overview**  
- **Gross Gold Reserves**:  
  - Stood at **116.1t** (IMF-reported) until September 2011.  
  - Surged to **716.3t by end-2020**, driven by government policies promoting gold in the financial system and reducing dollar reliance.  
- **Key Drivers**:  
  - Direct purchases from local/international markets.  
  - **Gold Monetisation Scheme**, **gold bonds**, **gold lease certificates**, and **gold swaps** with commercial banks.  

---

### **3. CBRT’s Own Reserves**  
- **Pre-2017**:  
  - Held **115.8t** until May 2017, with growth from commercial banks via the **Reserve Option Mechanism (ROM)**.  
- **Post-2017**:  
  - **Peaked at 440.6t in July 2020** (up from 115.8t in April 2017).  
  - **Sold 105t between August–December 2020**, reflecting economic instability and currency crisis.  
  - **Ended 2020 at 334.7t**, with gold mobilized to support the lira and repay international debt.  

---

### **4. Treasury’s Role in Gold Reserves**  
- **Gold Bonds and Sukuk**:  
  - Launched in **September 2017**, offering **interest-bearing gold bonds** and **gold sukuk** (Sharia-compliant bonds).  
  - Public deposited gold with **Ziraat Bank**, receiving bonds/sukuk redeemable in lira or physical gold.  
- **Volume Growth**:  
  - Reached **60t by end-2020**, rising to **94.7t by June 2021** through further auctions.  

---

### **5. Reserve Option Mechanism (ROM)**  
- **Purpose**:  
  - Introduced in **2011** to mitigate capital flow volatility and boost CBRT’s gold reserves.  
- **Key Features**:  
  - Banks could offset **30% of reserve liabilities** with gold (ROC), divided into tranches with varying ratios:  
    - **20%**: 1.2 ratio (e.g., 1.2t gold = 1t lira).  
    - **5%**: 1.7 ratio.  
    - **5%**: 2.2 ratio.  
  - **2016 Amendment**: Added a **5% tranche** for **scrap gold** (post-October 2016), with a **1:1 conversion** ratio.  
  - **2020–2021 Reforms**:  
    - ROC ceiling reduced to **20% (2020)** and **15% (2021)**.  
    - Scrap gold tranche expanded to **15%**.  
- **Impact**:  
  - Encouraged gold collection from the public, with interest rates on gold accounts peaking at **2.4% (2018)**, later dropping to **0.15–1%**.  
  - Banks now face higher reserve requirements (**18–22%** by 2020).  

---

### **6. Gold Swaps**  
- **Initiated in May 2019**:  
  - Commercial banks swapped gold with the CBRT for lira/foreign currency, increasing CBRT’s gold holdings.  
- **Growth**:  
  - Rose from **12.3t (May 2019)** to **123.5t by end-2020**, enhancing liquidity and foreign exchange management.  

---

### **Key Trends and Implications**  
- **Gold as a Strategic Tool**:  
  - Turkey’s gold reserves reflect a shift toward using gold to stabilize the currency and reduce dollar dependence.  
- **Economic Pressures**:  
  - Disposals in 2020 highlighted the CBRT’s role in mitigating economic crises, such as the lira’s decline.  
- **Innovative Mechanisms**:  
  - ROM and gold swaps demonstrate the CBRT’s adaptability in managing reserves through policy and financial instruments.  

--- 

This summary captures the evolution of Turkey’s CBRT gold reserves, the role of policy mechanisms, and the interplay between economic conditions and central bank strategy.

conversation.content: when using pydantic, can I create a class that extends BaseModel that automatically trunctions fields if they are too long? Assume the field in question is a string.

Yes, you can create a class that extends `BaseModel` in Pydantic and truncate string fields that exceed a certain length.

To do this, you can define a custom validator for the string fields in question using the `@validator` decorator. Within the validator function, you can check the length of the string field, and if it exceeds a certain length, truncate it to the desired length.

Here's an example:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, validator

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    text_field: str

    @validator('text_field')
    def truncate_text_field(cls, value):
        max_length = 10
        if len(value) > max_length:
            return value[:max_length]
        return value
```
In this example, the `MyModel` class has a `text_field` attribute that is a string. The `@validator` decorator is used to define a validator function called `truncate_text_field`. This function takes the value of the `text_field` attribute as an input.

The validator function checks the length of the `text_field` value and truncates it to a maximum length of 10 characters if it exceeds that length. If the length is less than or equal to 10, the original value is returned.

With this custom validator in place, any time you create an instance of `MyModel` and provide a value for the `text_field` attribute that exceeds the maximum length, it will be automatically truncated to the desired length.
Can I use the same validatr on multiple fields

Yes, you can use the same validator on multiple fields in Pydantic by specifying multiple field names within the `@validator` decorator.

Here's an example:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, validator

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    text_field1: str
    text_field2: str

    @validator('text_field1', 'text_field2')
    def truncate_text_field(cls, value):
        max_length = 10
        if len(value) > max_length:
            return value[:max_length]
        return value
```
In this example, the `MyModel` class has two attributes, `text_field1` and `text_field2`, both of which are strings. The `@validator` decorator is used to define a validator function called `truncate_text_field`, and the decorator is applied to both `text_field1` and `text_field2`.

The validator function checks the length of the input value and truncates it to a maximum length of 10 characters if it exceeds that length. If the length is less than or equal to 10, the original value is returned.

With this validator in place, any time you create an instance of `MyModel` and provide a value for either `text_field1` or `text_field2` that exceeds the maximum length, it will be automatically truncated to the desired length.

In my python code I'm using sqlalchemy to create records in the database. Currently one of the values is too large for a field I'm trying to write and the following error is being thrown.

(psycopg2.errors.StringDataRightTruncation) value too long for type character varying(256)
Is it possible to tell sqlalchemy to truncate the value when you attempt to write it?Save & SubmitCancel

Yes, it's possible to tell SQLAlchemy to truncate the value when you attempt to write it to a field that has a maximum length.

You can define the column of the database table using the `String` type with a `length` parameter that matches the maximum length of the column in the database, like this:
```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, String

class MyTable(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(length=256))
```
In this example, the `name` column is defined as a `String` with a `length` parameter of 256, which matches the maximum length of the column in the database.

When you try to insert a record with a `name` value that exceeds the maximum length, SQLAlchemy will truncate the value to fit the length of the column in the database.

For example, if you try to insert a record with a `name` value of "This is a very long name that exceeds the maximum length of the column", it will be automatically truncated to "This is a very long name that exceeds the maximum length of the c" to fit the length of the `name` column.

Alternatively, you can also manually truncate the value of the `name` attribute before inserting it into the database using Python's string slicing feature, like this:
```python
name = "This is a very long name that exceeds the maximum length of the column"
truncated_name = name[:256]  # truncate the name to 256 characters
my_record = MyTable(name=truncated_name)
session.add(my_record)
session.commit()
```
In this example, the `name` value is manually truncated to 256 characters using Python's string slicing feature, and the truncated value is used to create a new record in the `MyTable` table.

I'm using postgres as the backend database. It looks like postgres is throwing the error. Can I configure sqlalchemy to truncate a value that is too long for it's column if the underlying DB is postgres?

Yes, you can configure SQLAlchemy to automatically truncate values that are too long for a column when using a PostgreSQL database backend.

To do this, you can use the `server_default` argument in the `Column` definition to add a SQL expression that truncates the value to the maximum length of the column. Here's an example:
```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, String, text

class MyTable(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(length=256), server_default=text("substr(:name, 1, 256)"))
```
In this example, the `name` column is defined as a `String` with a `length` parameter of 256. The `server_default` argument is used to specify a SQL expression that truncates the value to 256 characters using the `substr` function in PostgreSQL.

When you try to insert a record with a `name` value that exceeds the maximum length, SQLAlchemy will automatically truncate the value to fit the length of the column in the database.

For example, if you try to insert a record with a `name` value of "This is a very long name that exceeds the maximum length of the column", it will be automatically truncated to "This is a very long name that exceeds the maximum length of the c" to fit the length of the `name` column.

Note that the `server_default` argument is only used to set the default value for the column when a new record is inserted. If you want to update an existing record with a truncated value, you will need to manually truncate the value in your Python code before updating the record.

response:
Yes, you can configure SQLAlchemy to **truncate values that are too long for a PostgreSQL column** when writing to the database. This can be achieved by **explicitly defining the column length in SQLAlchemy** and optionally using **custom validation logic** to truncate the data before it reaches the database. Here's how:

---

### ✅ **1. Define the Column with the Correct Length in SQLAlchemy**

PostgreSQL enforces column length constraints via `VARCHAR(n)` or `CHAR(n)`. SQLAlchemy's `String` type allows you to specify the maximum length of the column, which is passed to PostgreSQL:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, String, Integer
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base

Base = declarative_base()

class MyTable(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(length=256))  # Matches PostgreSQL's VARCHAR(256)
```

- **Important**: If you don't specify the `length` parameter, PostgreSQL may not enforce the maximum length (e.g., if the column is defined as `TEXT` or `VARCHAR` without a length). Always explicitly define the length to ensure consistency between SQLAlchemy and PostgreSQL.

---

### ✅ **2. Use SQLAlchemy's `@validates` Decorator to Truncate Values**

If you want to **ensure the value is truncated in Python before sending it to the database**, use SQLAlchemy's `@validates` decorator. This is useful if you want to handle truncation in your application logic rather than relying solely on the database.

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, String, Integer
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import validates

Base = declarative_base()

class MyTable(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(length=256))  # Define the column with max length

    @validates('name')
    def truncate_name(self, value):
        max_length = 256
        if len(value) > max_length:
            return value[:max_length]
        return value
```

- **What this does**:
  - The `@validates('name')` decorator ensures that the `name` field is validated before being assigned.
  - If the string exceeds 256 characters, it is truncated to 256 characters.
  - This prevents the `StringDataRightTruncation` error by ensuring the value is valid before it reaches the database.

---

### ✅ **3. Use SQLAlchemy's `String` with a Default Length (Optional)**

If you're unsure of the exact maximum length, you can define the column with a default length (e.g., 256) that matches PostgreSQL's default behavior:

```python
name = Column(String(256))
```

This ensures that SQLAlchemy and PostgreSQL agree on the column's maximum length, avoiding mismatches.

---

### ✅ **4. Combine with Database Constraints (Recommended)**

While truncating in Python is helpful, it's **better practice to let the database enforce constraints**. This ensures data integrity even if the application logic is flawed. PostgreSQL will throw an error if the string is too long, but SQLAlchemy can be configured to **ignore or handle the error**.

To suppress the error, you can catch the `psycopg2.errors.StringDataRightTruncation` exception in your application:

```python
from psycopg2 import errors
from sqlalchemy.exc import SQLAlchemyError

try:
    session.add(new_record)
    session.commit()
except SQLAlchemyError as e:
    if isinstance(e.orig, errors.StringDataRightTruncation):
        # Handle truncation gracefully
        print("Value too long for column; truncating...")
        # Optionally update the record with the truncated value
        new_record.name = new_record.name[:256]
        session.add(new_record)
        session.commit()
    else:
        raise
```

---

### ✅ **Summary**

| Approach | When to Use |
|---------|-------------|
| **Define column length** | Ensure PostgreSQL and SQLAlchemy agree on the maximum length. |
| **Use `@validates`** | Truncate values in Python before sending to the database. |
| **Catch and handle errors** | Gracefully handle truncation if the database enforces constraints. |

---

### ✅ **Best Practice**

- Always define the column length explicitly in SQLAlchemy to align with PostgreSQL.
- Use `@validates` to truncate values in Python if needed.
- Let PostgreSQL enforce constraints for data integrity, and handle errors gracefully if necessary.

This approach ensures your application remains robust while maintaining consistency between your code and the database.

In summary, HPPD (hallucinogen-persisting perception disorder) is a condition characterized by persistent perceptual disturbances following the cessation of hallucinogenic drug use. It has been predominantly associated with the use of LSD, but cases have also been reported after the use of other serotonergic hallucinogens, cannabis, and novel psychoactive substances (NPS). Studies on the clinical manifestation and psychopathology of HPPD have reported various visual disturbances and triggers, with some differences between the two types of HPPD.

The treatment of HPPD remains challenging due to the limited number of published reports and the lack of controlled studies. Some medications, such as clonazepam and lamotrigine, have shown promise in ameliorating HPPD symptomatology. However, other medications, such as risperidone, have shown mixed results. As the understanding of HPPD and its associated substances continue to evolve, further research is needed to clarify the pathogenesis and etiology of HPPD and to identify effective treatment strategies.

response:
**Conclusion and Future Directions**  
The clinical and pharmacological landscape of HPPD remains complex and multifaceted, with emerging challenges posed by the proliferation of novel psychoactive substances (NPS). While LSD has historically been the most frequently implicated agent in HPPD cases, the increasing availability of synthetic compounds—such as NBOMe derivatives, synthetic cannabinoids (SCs), and new LSD analogs—has expanded the spectrum of substances associated with persistent perceptual disturbances. These NPS often exhibit enhanced potency and altered pharmacokinetics, which may predispose users to prolonged or intensified HPPD symptoms, even after cessation of use. The mechanisms underlying this phenomenon likely involve interactions between serotonergic and dopaminergic pathways, as well as neuroplastic changes in visual and perceptual processing regions of the brain.  

Future research should prioritize the development of standardized diagnostic criteria for HPPD, particularly in the context of NPS use, to improve clinical recognition and differentiate HPPD from other psychiatric or neurological conditions. Large-scale, longitudinal studies with rigorous methodologies—such as double-blind, placebo-controlled trials—are essential to elucidate the efficacy of pharmacological interventions, including atypical antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, and glutamate-modulating agents. Additionally, the role of genetic and environmental factors in susceptibility to HPPD warrants further exploration, as individual variability in drug metabolism and neural responses may influence outcomes.  

Policy and public health initiatives must also address the growing risk of HPPD associated with NPS, emphasizing harm reduction strategies, education on substance use risks, and improved access to psychiatric care for affected individuals. As the field advances, interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, researchers, and policymakers will be critical to mitigating the burden of HPPD in an era of evolving drug use patterns.  

**References**  
(Continuation of citations would follow the existing format, ensuring all referenced studies and authors are properly acknowledged.)  

**Author Contributions**  
LO and FS conceived the topic and structured the manuscript, while AG and GP conducted the literature review and analysis. JC and DB contributed to the screening of studies and manuscript preparation. FS served as the senior study reviewer, ensuring methodological rigor and coherence throughout the work.  

**Funding**  
This study was supported by grants from the European Commission’s Drug Prevention and Information Programme (2014–2016, contract no. JUST/2013/DPIP/AG/4823) and the EU-MADNESS project. Additional funding was provided by the EU Commission-targeted call on cross-border law enforcement cooperation in drug trafficking (JUST/2013/ISEC/DRUGS/AG/6429) and the EPS/NPS project, which focuses on enhancing police skills concerning novel psychoactive substances.  

---  
This continuation synthesizes the existing discussion, emphasizing the interplay between NPS, HPPD pathogenesis, and the need for targeted research and policy measures. It maintains the academic tone while addressing gaps in the literature and outlining actionable steps for future investigation.

conversation.content: Do you know what a chick tract is?

Yes, I'm familiar with Chick tracts. Chick tracts are small comic book tracts created by Jack Chick and his publishing company, Chick Publications. They typically feature strong religious themes and are used to spread Christian evangelism and to promote a conservative interpretation of the Bible. Chick tracts often include controversial and provocative content, including depictions of hell, demons, and various perceived sins, and have been the subject of much criticism and debate over the years.

CHAPTER FIFTEEN
 RULE #6
 Crawl Before You Walk, Walk Before You Run
 From Jeremy
As you know by now, the essence of the protocol is lifelong behavioral change. That means changing the way you carry yourself and move (the neutral spine and all that). But it also means adopting and staying with our carefully designed (and progressive) exercise regimen. It is designed for back pain and it is essential for a permanent cure. And you have to do it correctly. There is an awful lot of text and a bunch of pictures about exercise in this book. But it’s not because you have to do so many exercises; there really aren’t that many. It’s because we want you to do them right. Remember, you are far more likely to do these exercises with the compensatory patterns you have been doing for years unless you read closely and concentrate on doing them the right way. Doing them the wrong way will just make you better at doing bad things. 
One great key to doing them right is to go slow in the learning process. Whether you’re the type or not, you have to take it step-by-step. This is a healing process, and you cannot rush a healing process; it has its own rhythm and you have to follow it. Roll out these exercises slowly over time, progressing from one level to the next only when you “get” the level you are on and are ready to move up. 
Remember, it is these daily exercises that—more than anything else—are going to give most of you a significant and permanent reduction in pain and tightness. Other steps get you past the immediate pain. These steps make the change permanent. 
Be Still to Heal; Be Still to Stay Well 
One of the main goals of changing your movement patterns is to make the “be still to heal” concept (Rule #2, in Chapter 7) part of your daily life. Once you have achieved an initial fix, you don’t have to be as still. But it is a great idea to learn to move with relative stillness (without moving your lumbar spine too much) all the time. 
This is a big change for most of you, and you have to take it in steps; as I say, you have to crawl before you walk. The first step in learning to keep your lumbar spine still routinely is simply to learn to march in place, with a neutral spine. (This is the “crawl” part of the process; it may seem too easy to bother with. It is not.) The goal is to learn to march in place with no lower spine movement. Then you can move on to other movements in this chapter.
Key tip: If any of these movements hurt, take a step back to the last thing you could do without pain (marching in place, for instance). Stay with that for a few more days and then try to progress again. In theory, if you can march in place without pain, you can walk without pain and move without pain. It’s just a matter of building up your core, turning on the right muscles, and learning to use those muscles to move in the right way. Whenever you have difficulty, go back to the previous step and start again. 
Okay, you’ve done marching in place without moving your lower spine. Once that feels relatively simple, try doing this while walking. Things become a little murkier here because there are many factors at play. Because you are now weight-bearing, you need the support of important muscle groups like the glutes and latissimus dorsi (lats) to support your spine, in addition to the muscles we talked about in the core. If you can’t do this at first without pain, follow the advice in the book for a while, and it will come once you have developed enough strength to support your spine while standing. 
Walking with Neutral Spine 
Learning to walk with a neutral spine means applying the same mechanics of your Slow March with Neutral Spine exercise to an upright, weight-bearing position. Almost everyone can improve their ability to walk without pain if they can do the slow march exercise on the ground. The distance you can walk without pain, or without significant increases in pain, will increase as you build core and gluteal strength and endurance by doing the exercises in this book. To get started on increasing your ability to walk with decreased pain, follow these directions:
Step 1: Stand with good posture. Stand tall, shoulders back, head over your shoulders. Think of sticking your chest out and pulling your shoulder blades down and back. Make sure you aren’t leaning forward. Tilt your pelvis back and forth (just like you did on the floor earlier), and stop where your lower back feels the most comfortable. 
Step 2: Use your core to lock your neutral spine in place. 
Step 3: Now try to walk while maintaining the position of your lower back and with the good posture you just set. It can help to put one hand on your stomach and one on your low back to feel for movement. Once you feel like you’ve mastered maintaining neutral spine while walking, start to swing your arms from the shoulders, arm to opposite leg. This means if your right leg is swinging forward, your left arm should be as well. Make sure to always swing your arms from the shoulders as you walk; this helps dissipate force away from the spine. This movement takes time to master so don’t get frustrated. Keep practicing. It gets easier after you have built endurance and strength in the muscles that support the spine. 
Step 4: If you sense your back is about to hurt, stop and have a seat for a few minutes and give your back a break. Then try walking a little longer. Over time, the period between these sitting breaks should increase, allowing you to walk farther without pain.
Now let’s move on to more complex movements. To make it easier (and to improve your chances of success) we are going to break those more complex movements down into pieces and then put them all together at the end. We will start with movement from the hips, a very basic move in all our lives all the time. Let’s start with hip-hinging. Hip-hinging is a way to bend forward without stressing (i.e., bending) the spine. Done right, the axis of movement is in the hips, not the low back. Your low back should be in a protected neutral position with the core engaged. As you bend forward, your low back doesn’t round or move. Let’s look at a picture of hip-hinging and a picture of lumbar flexion (low back bending); one is good, the other is bad. This is simple stuff, but it is very hard to get over your ancient, bad habits. Hard but essential. 

Hip-Hinge with Neutral Spine
Here are a couple of pictures showing you the right way to hinge (on the left) and the wrong way (on the right). Notice the very slightly curved lower back in the hip-hinge picture on the left: no movement. Excellent! The pic on the right has quite a lot of spine movement; don’t do that. When you first try this movement, it may feel awkward, and you may assume that you cannot reach as far forward with good posture. Not so. You’ll soon realize that you can move just as far forward, using only your hips, as you did before, using your back. You don’t lose anything and you avoid a ton of pain. Over time you will gain mobility. The reason is that moving with your lower back eventually causes pain and stiffness in your back. But moving from your hips will not have that result. Let’s practice doing it right. 
Right Wrong

Step 1: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Set your posture: Find neutral spine, brace the core, shoulders back, stand as tall as you can without pain and without breaking neutral spine. 
Step 3: Bend your knees slightly. 
Step 4: Feel the two bones sticking out of the sides of your hips. These are called your greater trochanters. Picture a pole going through these two points, passing right through your pelvis. This is your imaginary axis of movement.
Step 5: Now place one hand on your stomach and the other on your back. On your back, put your fingers on those little bones sticking out (spinous processes). You are putting your hands here to feel for any unwanted movement when you hip-hinge. 
Step 6: Without moving anything in the core (you shouldn’t feel those little bones in your back moving), slowly bend forward on that imaginary axis of movement through the hip bones while sticking your buttocks out behind you a little bit. If you feel your stomach or back move, stop, reset, and try again.

Step 7: Go only as far forward as you can without moving your back. For many of you, your hamstrings (back of legs) are going to be a limiting factor in your range of motion in the beginning. Don’t worry if you can’t go too far. Your hamstrings will loosen up in time if you follow all of the advice in this book. 
Step 8: Slowly return to the starting position without moving your back or stomach. 
Step 9: This should not hurt your low back. If it does, reset your stance, find neutral spine, brace your core. Not hurting? Now try it again with a very small range of motion. Stay within the range of motion that doesn’t hurt. Slowly increase your range of motion over time—it will come. 
Adding Rotation
Now let’s get a little more complex. Let’s add rotation to the bend. Hip-hinging correctly allows you to move forward and backward without pain. Now let’s learn to move side to side. We are talking rotation. Again, think about moving from the hips. It’s easiest to visualize proper rotation if you think about your waist. We don’t want any movement at the waist. Try to picture your rib cage being locked on to your pelvis. That’s what you want. Pretend that there are no joints between your ribs and pelvis, for this one. 
Full-Body Rotation
The chest, navel, and hips all move together. This means there is no (or very little) movement in the spine. The body rotates by moving the hips, knees, and pelvis. If the hips and pelvis stay facing forward while the rib cage moves, the movement comes from the waist, and therefore the lumbar spine. This type of movement can wear down the discs and joints in the spine over time, especially when additional load is applied (like unloading the dishwasher for instance). Just like hip-hinging, rotation done properly doesn’t cause you to lose mobility and will improve athletic performance. Let’s give it a try. Once again, the new movement is not that hard. But you are trying to unlearn a lifetime of moving with your spine. That is hard. 

Step 1: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Set your posture: Find neutral spine, brace the core, shoulders back, stand as tall as you can without pain and without breaking neutral spine. 
Step 3: Bend your knees slightly. 
Step 4: Place your hands on the top of your pelvis with your fingers pointed forward. This hand position is to help you visualize movement of the pelvis as you get used to this movement. 
Step 5: Keep the knees bent and loose but the core engaged, and try to turn a little to the right by shifting the knees and hips. Your fingers, navel, hip bones, chest bone, and face should all point the same direction. It’s okay if you pivot the feet a little bit. 

Step 6: Now try to the left.
Step 7: Slowly increase your range of motion by bending more at the knees. 
Step 8: Then increase your range of motion even more by pivoting your feet. 
Step 9: At all times and especially at the end of the movement, your chest bone, head, navel, and hips should be in alignment. 
TROUBLESHOOTING
Knee pain: Stay lighter on your feet\ and pivot the ball of the inside foot (the side you are moving away from) as you rotate. 
Torso Rotation with Hip-Hinge and Squat
Now let’s try to put those two movements—the hip-hinge and the rotation—together with a squat for a realistic, three-dimensional movement that allows you to accomplish everyday tasks (think unloading the dishwasher, putting up groceries) without stressing your back. You are going to rotate and bend down (hip-hinge), then come back up to the other side and extend. 
Notice that within that entire chain of movement, the person in the drawing does not really move his spine (look at the waist if you’re having trouble visualizing that). The rib cage remains locked onto the pelvis the entire time. These pictures show you that you can go almost 180 degrees from side to side, stooping as low as you can and then extending as high as your hands can go, without compromising your back. Let’s try it.
Step 1: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Find neutral spine and engage your core to lock it in. 
Step 3: Stand with good posture. 
Step 4: Rotate torso to the right without twisting the waist as before.

Step 5: Pivot the inside foot (left foot, in this case) as you turn.
Step 6: After initiating the rotation, start to hinge at the hips, bending forward. Remember not to round your back. 

Step 7: Go only as low and as far to the right as you can without twisting at the waist or rounding your low back. You will now be in a squat/split squat position.

Step 8: Reach out with your arms as if you were picking something up off of the floor. 

Step 9: Bring the arms back in and re-center your weight over your buttocks and feet. 
Step 10: Using your glutes, lift yourself out of the lowered position, pushing with your right glutes as you rotate back to midline while hinging back up through the hips. 

Step 11: As you swing past midline (without twisting at the waist!), pivot your right foot, continuing to use the right glutes, and extend your arms up toward the ceiling as if you were putting something on a shelf. 
Step 12: Return to the starting position without twisting at the waist. Try this in front of a mirror several times. 
Step 13: Then try this movement on the opposite side of your body. 
TROUBLESHOOTING
• Knee pain: If your knees hurt as you start to rotate the torso, be lighter on your feet. Allow the feet to pivot a little bit. If the knees hurt when you are hinged forward at the bottom of the movement, drop your buttocks back more so that you are in a squat position (think of a baseball catcher) and lessen your range of motion. Try doing small movements and increase your range of motion gradually.
• Back pain: If your back hurts with this one, it is likely you are twisting at the waist, rounding the back, or don’t have the gluteal strength yet to perform this movement to its full range of motion. Do a little investigating: Can you hip-hinge without pain? If so, that’s not the issue. Can you do the torso rotation without pain? Can you squat without pain? If you can do all three without pain then it is likely you are having difficulty putting all of these moves together without sacrificing form on one of them. Try doing this movement in very small pieces in front of a mirror or loved one, and gradually increase your range of motion over time. If any one piece causes pain, work on that one until you can do it pain-free, and then come back and try this movement again. 
Remember, these movements must become habits. That sounds daunting but it really isn’t that hard. Once your back starts to feel better, it will let you know when you move the wrong way and you’ll avoid that at all costs.
This chapter discusses the importance of adopting and following the exercise regimen designed for back pain relief in

response:
**Chapter 15 Summary: Rule #6 – "Crawl Before You Walk, Walk Before You Run"**  
This chapter focuses on **lifelong behavioral change** to heal and prevent back pain through **progressive, correct movement patterns**. The core message is that healing requires patience, precision, and gradual progression, starting with foundational exercises before advancing to more complex movements. Here's a structured breakdown:

---

### **Key Concepts & Objectives**  
1. **Healing Through Movement**  
   - The protocol emphasizes **changing movement habits** to reduce pain and prevent recurrence.  
   - Daily exercises are critical for **permanent change**, not just temporary relief.  
   - **"Be still to heal"** (Rule #2) is reinforced: moving with minimal lumbar spine motion is essential.  

2. **Progressive Learning**  
   - **Start simple**: Begin with "crawling" (basic movements like marching in place) before advancing to "walking" (more complex tasks).  
   - **Avoid rushing**: Healing has its own rhythm. Progress only when you’ve mastered the current level.  

---

### **Core Exercises & Techniques**  
#### **1. Marching in Place with Neutral Spine**  
- **Goal**: Learn to move without lumbar spine motion.  
- **Steps**:  
  1. Stand tall with a neutral spine (pelvis tilted slightly back).  
  2. March in place, keeping your lower back still.  
  3. If pain occurs, regress to a simpler movement (e.g., marching) until pain subsides.  
- **Why**: Builds core strength and teaches the body to move from the hips, not the spine.  

#### **2. Hip-Hinging (Forward Bend)**  
- **Goal**: Bend forward without stressing the lumbar spine.  
- **Steps**:  
  1. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, core engaged, and posture neutral.  
  2. Bend at the hips, keeping the lower back still, and stick out your buttocks.  
  3. Use hands to monitor movement: no motion in the spine, only at the hips.  
- **Why**: Prevents lower back strain and improves hip flexibility over time.  

#### **3. Rotation (Side-to-Side Movement)**  
- **Goal**: Move side-to-side without twisting the waist or rounding the spine.  
- **Steps**:  
  1. Keep knees slightly bent and core engaged.  
  2. Shift hips and knees to rotate the torso, keeping the rib cage "locked" to the pelvis.  
  3. Align chest, navel, hips, and head in the same direction.  
- **Why**: Reduces spinal load during daily tasks like reaching or lifting.  

#### **4. Combined Movement: Hip-Hinge + Rotation + Squat**  
- **Goal**: Perform a full, three-dimensional movement (e.g., unloading a dishwasher) without back pain.  
- **Steps**:  
  1. Rotate torso to one side, hinge at the hips, and squat.  
  2. Reach forward, then return to center, and extend upward.  
  3. Avoid twisting the waist or rounding the back.  
- **Why**: Simulates real-life movements safely, integrating all previous skills.  

---

### **Troubleshooting Common Issues**  
- **Knee Pain**:  
  - Pivot on the inside of the foot (away from the direction of rotation).  
  - Use a squat position (like a baseball catcher) if pain persists.  
- **Back Pain**:  
  - Check for waist twisting or rounded back.  
  - Ensure core strength and proper form.  
  - Break movements into smaller steps and focus on one at a time.  

---

### **Key Takeaways**  
- **Start small**: Begin with marching, then progress to hip-hinge and rotation.  
- **Prioritize form**: Correct execution is more important than speed or range of motion.  
- **Listen to your body**: If pain occurs, regress to a simpler movement and rebuild strength.  
- **Make it a habit**: Consistency over time will retrain your body to move pain-free.  

By following these steps, you’ll gradually recondition your muscles, improve posture, and reduce reliance on harmful compensatory patterns. Healing is a process—patience and precision are your allies. 🌱💪

---

### **Welcome, Business Owner!**  
You’re not alone in wanting to create stunning videos for your social media. Whether you’re a small business owner, freelancer, or entrepreneur, video is the secret weapon to engage your audience, build trust, and drive sales. But let’s be honest—video can feel overwhelming. That’s why we’ve created this **free gift guide** to help you unlock the power of video, step by step.  

This guide is packed with actionable tips, tools, and tricks to help you **shoot like a pro and edit like a wizard**—without needing a degree in film or a million-dollar budget. Let’s dive in!  

---

## **Part 1: Why Video Matters for Your Business**  
Before we get into the nitty-gritty, let’s talk about **why video is your new best friend**.  

### **1.1 Engagement is King (and Queen)**  
Studies show that **videos on social media get 10x more engagement** than text or images. Why? Because video tells a story, builds connection, and keeps people scrolling.  

### **1.2 Trust & Credibility**  
When you share behind-the-scenes footage, product demos, or customer testimonials, you’re humanizing your brand. People trust businesses that feel real.  

### **1.3 SEO & Visibility**  
Platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube prioritize video content in search results. The more videos you post, the more likely your audience will find you.  

### **1.4 Sales & Conversions**  
A 2023 report found that **videos can increase conversion rates by up to 80%**. Use videos to showcase your product, explain your process, or share success stories.  

---

## **Part 2: Shooting Like a Pro (Without a Team)**  
You don’t need a Hollywood budget to create great video. Here’s how to shoot like a pro with minimal tools.  

### **2.1 Start with the Right Equipment**  
**Your phone is your best friend.** Modern smartphones have cameras that rival professional gear. But if you want to up your game:  

- **Lenses**: Invest in a **prime lens** (like a 50mm) for sharper, more cinematic shots.  
- **Tripod**: Use a tripod to avoid shaky footage. (A phone tripod is under $20!)  
- **Lighting**: Natural light is free and best. Use a lamp or ring light for indoor shots.  

### **2.2 Plan Your Shots**  
Before filming, ask:  
- *What message do I want to convey?*  
- *What’s the story I’m telling?*  
- *What’s the best angle to highlight my product/service?*  

**Pro Tip**: Use a **shot list** (even a simple list on your phone) to stay organized.  

### **2.3 Master the Basics**  
- **Rule of Thirds**: Imagine a grid over your frame. Place your subject at the intersection points for balance.  
- **Lighting**: Avoid harsh shadows. Use a reflector or soft light source.  
- **Audio**: A poor voiceover or background noise can ruin your video. Use a **USB mic** (like the Blue Yeti) or record in a quiet space.  

### **2.4 Get Creative with Angles**  
- **Low angle**: Makes your subject look powerful.  
- **High angle**: Adds a sense of intimacy.  
- **Close-up**: Focus on details (like a product’s texture).  
- **Wide shot**: Show your workspace or environment.  

### **2.5 Film Like a Storyteller**  
- **Start strong**: Grab attention in the first 3 seconds.  
- **Keep it short**: 60 seconds is ideal for social media.  
- **End with a call-to-action**: “Tag a friend who needs this!” or “Click the link in bio.”  

---

## **Part 3: Editing Like a Wizard (Without a Degree)**  
Editing can feel like a maze, but it’s simpler than you think. Here’s how to turn raw footage into a polished video.  

### **3.1 Choose the Right Software**  
**Free options**:  
- **Canva**: Great for quick edits and templates.  
- **CapCut** (formerly VivaVideo): Perfect for TikTok and Instagram Reels.  
- **DaVinci Resolve**: Free, powerful, and ideal for advanced users.  

**Paid options**:  
- **Adobe Premiere Pro** (for pro-level editing).  
- **Final Cut Pro** (Mac users).  

### **3.2 Editing Tips for Beginners**  
- **Trim and cut**: Remove unnecessary parts. Keep it concise.  
- **Add text**: Use captions or on-screen text to highlight key points.  
- **Music**: Use royalty-free music (try Epidemic Sound or Artlist).  
- **Transitions**: Use subtle transitions (like fade-ins/fade-outs) to keep the flow smooth.  

### **3.3 Advanced Techniques (Once You’re Confident)**  
- **Split-screen**: Show two perspectives (e.g., a customer using your product).  
- **Slow motion**: Highlight a product’s details or a key moment.  
- **Green screen**: Use a green backdrop to replace the background with a virtual one.  

### **3.4 Export Like a Pro**  
- **Resolution**: 1080p is standard for most platforms.  
- **Format**: MP4 is universal and compatible with all platforms.  
- **Bitrate**: Higher is better (but don’t overdo it—15-20 Mbps is ideal).  

---

## **Part 4: Tools & Resources to Level Up Your Video Game**  
Here are some free and paid tools to help you crush your video content:  

### **4.1 Free Tools**  
- **Lumen5**: Turn blog posts into videos.  
- **Renderforest**: Create templates for social media.  
- **PicsArt**: Edit videos and add effects.  

### **4.2 Paid Tools (Worth the Investment)**  
- **Adobe Premiere Pro**: Industry-standard editing software.  
- **Tubebuddy**: Analyze your video performance.  
- **Descript**: Voice-to-text editing (perfect for scripts).  

### **4.3 Templates & Stock Footage**  
- **Piktochart**: Free video templates.  
- **Pixabay**: Free stock footage and music.  
- **Videvo**: Royalty-free videos for your projects.  

---

## **Part 5: A Step-by-Step Checklist for Your Next Video**  
Use this checklist to ensure your video is on point:  

1. **Plan your content**: What message do you want to share?  
2. **Choose your equipment**: Phone, tripod, and lighting.  
3. **Shoot in different angles**: Close-up, wide shot, and low/high angles.  
4. **Record clear audio**: Use a mic or speak clearly.  
5. **Edit your footage**: Trim, add text, and music.  
6. **Export and upload**: Use the right format and resolution.  
7. **Add a call-to-action**: Encourage engagement or clicks.  

---

## **Part 6: Bonus Tips for Success**  
- **Post consistently**: Aim for 3–5 videos per week.  
- **Engage with your audience**: Respond to comments and ask questions.  
- **Analyze your performance**: Use insights to improve your next video.  
- **Collaborate**: Partner with influencers or other businesses for cross-promotion.  

---

## **Final Thoughts: You’ve Got This!**  
Creating video content doesn’t have to be stressful. With the right tools, planning, and practice, you can produce videos that resonate with your audience and grow your business.  

Remember: **Consistency > Perfection**. Even a short, authentic video is better than a polished one you never post.  

---

```
aoc22day1\_stream\_iterators.cpp:63:31: error: no matching function for call to 'bind'

 ^~~~~~~~~
C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community\VC\Tools\MSVC\14.34.31933\include\functional:2024:60: note: candidate template ignored: couldn't infer template argument '\_Fx'
\_NODISCARD \_CONSTEXPR20 \_Binder<\_Unforced, \_Fx, \_Types...> bind(\_Fx&& \_Func, \_Types&&... \_Args) {
 ^
C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community\VC\Tools\MSVC\14.34.31933\include\functional:2029:55: note: candidate template ignored: couldn't infer template argument '\_Ret'
\_NODISCARD \_CONSTEXPR20 \_Binder<\_Ret, \_Fx, \_Types...> bind(\_Fx&& \_Func, \_Types&&... \_Args) {
```
What's wrong?

I've got a question:
Why is the `&` in `(const int&)` required?

The `&` in `(const int&)` is not strictly required in this case, but it's commonly used to indicate that the argument passed to the bound function is a reference to a `const int`.

In the case of the `push_back` method of `std::vector`, the argument type is `const int&`, which is a reference to a `const int`. When we bind this method to a callable object using `std::bind`, we need to specify the type of the argument using `std::placeholders::_1`, as we did in the previous example.

When we call the bound function with an argument, `std::bind` will pass that argument to the original method. If we pass an `int` instead of a `const int&`, `std::bind` will automatically convert it to a `const int&`. However, if we pass a non-const reference or a pointer, the compiler will generate an error because `push_back` expects a `const int&` argument.

By specifying `(const int&)` in the call to `std::bind`, we indicate that the argument passed to the bound function should be a reference to a `const int`. This can be useful in cases where we want to make it clear that the bound function expects a `const int&` argument and avoid accidental modifications to the original data.
